comments.text: The smallest known pair of starting values for which a Graham-type Lucas sequence exists, was gradually brought down by Knuth, Wilf, Nicol and Vsemirnov, with the latter holding the current record with $a_0 = 106276436867$ and $a_1 = 35256392432$ (despite Erdős and Graham writing 'it would be very surprising if a pair existed with both components less than $10^{20}$' in [ErGr80]!). So these starting values lead to a Lucas sequence which, thanks to a covering system, only contains composite values. Fun fact: in what feels like an hommage to Graham, the papers by Knuth, Nicol and Vsemirnov all have almost the same title A (new) Fibonacci-like sequence of composite numbers, which was also the title of Graham's original paper. Wilf's example can be found on the OEIS as A083216. As for a Lucas sequence of composite integers without an underlying covering system (i.e. the question at hand), this was conjecturally solved by Ismailescu and Son in this paper (appropriately titled A new kind of Fibonacci-like sequence of composite numbers), where you can find references to the aforementioned papers as well. With $p = 1$, $q$ a $129$-digit monstrosity (see Theorem 3 on page 6 of their paper), $a_0 = p^2 + q^2$, $a_1 = 2pq + q^2$ and $a_{n+2} = a_{n+1} + a_n$ for all $n \ge 0$, Ismailescu and Son prove the following: 1) There exists a positive integer $m$ (for which all its prime factors are smaller than $a_0$) such that $\gcd(m, a_{2n}) > 1$ for all $n \ge 0$. That is, all even-indexed terms are composite thanks to a covering system. 2) For all $n \ge 0$ we have $a_{2n+1} = (pF_n + qF_{n+1})(pL_n + qL_{n+1})$ with $F_n$ and $L_n$ the Fibonacci and Lucas numbers respectively. In particular, all odd-indexed terms are composite too. Now, as for these odd-indexed terms, there seems to be no reason to believe that a covering system is responsible here as well. However, they are not actually able to prove the non-existence of an $m$ such that $\gcd(m, a_{2n+1}) > 1$ holds for all $n \ge 0$. So that is why one could say that this problem is conjecturally solved. I do wonder if it's possible to use the ideas in Hough's theorem and follow-up work, to reduce deciding whether a covering system is responsible for the odd-indexed terms in the above example, to a finite computation. PS. Due to Dogmachine's comment the assumption on the coprimality of $a_0$ and $a_1$ was removed. However, this assumption is of course important for Graham's example. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

It is likely solved then. This problem is of the same type as that of proving that the sequence $(5.2^n+1)$ contains arbitrarily large subsequences of pairwise relatively prime terms, which was asked by Michael Filaseta.
We may the omit the coprime condition here without any problem. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
